nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated and non-migratory; juveniles common in tide pools and intertidal areas, adults found over shallow coral and rocky reefs typically at 0–10 m and down to about 20–40 m

diet.notes: Accepts a wide variety of meaty and herbivorous foods including flake, pellet, frozen and freeze-dried preparations. In the wild has been documented feeding on algae, zooplankton (copepods, shrimp larvae), invertebrate larvae, tunicates, small crustaceans, and small fishes; juveniles have also been observed cleaning parasites from sea turtles.

summary: A hardy Atlantic damselfish with five bold black bars on a silvery-white body and a yellow dorsal wash; generally beginner-friendly but tends to become territorial as it matures.
careNotes: The Sergeant Major (Abudefduf saxatilis) is an Atlantic and Caribbean damselfish often confused with its Indo-Pacific sister species Abudefduf vaigiensis — LiveAquaria's 'Sergeant Major Damselfish' listing in fact uses the A. vaigiensis scientific name; in the trade the two are sold interchangeably and husbandry advice is essentially identical, but Atlantic-collected stock is A. saxatilis. Generally considered hardy and beginner-friendly, but it has been reported to grow noticeably more territorial and aggressive as it matures, especially toward conspecifics and other damselfish. It is best kept singly in moderate tanks, or in groups of five or more in larger systems where aggression can be diffused across multiple territories. Tank-size recommendations range widely across sources (LiveAquaria suggests 75 US gal / 284 L, hobbyist sources 40–55 gal / 150–208 L) — a roomier 300 L (~80 gal) footprint is recommended here because the species is an active mid-water swimmer that needs open space and benefits from territorial buffer when housed with tankmates. Temperature guidance averaged across sources: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), while FishBase records sea temperatures of about 19.9–28.1°C across the species' range — keepers are generally advised to aim for a stable tropical Atlantic profile around 24–27°C. Reef-safe and will not harm corals or sessile invertebrates, but may pester smaller mobile invertebrates and outcompete shyer tankmates at feeding time.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pair bonding during breeding. Males establish and aggressively defend nesting sites on rocks or reef substrate; females deposit large adhesive egg masses (~200,000 eggs reported in field studies) that the male guards and aerates for about a week until hatching. Breeding in home aquaria is uncommon and largely restricted to very large, well-established systems.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Collection root used because the direct product page (sergeant-major-damselfish-1) lists this species under the Indo-Pacific congener A. vaigiensis; husbandry values (75 gal min, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, omnivore, semi-aggressive, reef-safe) were extracted from the listing.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, native Atlantic range, habitat, max adult size (22.9 cm TL, common 15 cm SL), temperature envelope (19.9-28.1 C), diet composition, and reproduction. FishBase confirms strictly-Atlantic distribution and notes Indo-Pacific A. vaigiensis as the replacement congener.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Pomacentridae), order (Blenniiformes), max size (22.9 cm), depth range, diet, reproduction (males guard ~200,000 eggs for ~1 week), and IUCN status (Least Concern).

additional.name: Hobbyist sources (aggregated web search)
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank-size spread: LiveAquaria 75 gal / 284 L, TankTag 55 gal / 208 L, saltwaterfish.com 40 gal / 150 L; biased larger to 300 L per playbook tank-size rule given active-swimmer and territorial prose. Lifespan reported as 10+ years.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous demersal egg layer; males guard nests of ~200,000 adhesive eggs on rock substrate for about a week. Rarely bred in home aquaria.
